About Contingency Billing

Attorney takes a percentage of the settlement or verdict (typically 33-40%). You pay nothing upfront. Common for personal injury, employment discrimination, and some consumer protection cases.

What's Typically Included
✓
Case evaluation
✓
Investigation and evidence gathering
✓
Medical records collection
✓
Insurance negotiation
✓
Court filing if needed
✓
Additional negotiation rounds
✓
Expert consultation coordination

Actual scope varies by attorney and firm. Always confirm what is and is not included before signing an engagement letter.

Factors That Affect Your Cost
Attorney experience: Senior partners charge 2-3x more than junior associates
Firm size: Large firms charge 30-100% more than solo practitioners for similar work
Location: Major metro areas (NYC, LA, Chicago, SF) cost significantly more than rural areas
Case complexity: Discovery, expert witnesses, and multiple court appearances add cost
Opposing party: An uncooperative opposing party can double or triple costs
Next Steps
Get free consultations from 2-3 attorneys to compare pricing and fit
Verify credentials on your state bar association website
Ask for a written fee agreement before hiring
Confirm what happens if the case takes longer than expected
Ask about payment plans -- many attorneys offer them
Check reviews on Avvo, Martindale-Hubbell, and Google
Clarify whether costs (filing fees, expert witnesses) are deducted before or after the contingency percentage
Ask what happens if you lose -- some firms still charge for costs
Ways to Reduce Legal Costs
Many attorneys offer free initial consultations -- always ask
Consider unbundled legal services (attorney handles only specific tasks)
Legal aid organizations provide free help for income-qualifying individuals
Law school clinics offer low-cost legal help supervised by licensed attorneys
Online legal services (for simple matters like basic wills or LLC formation) cost 60-80% less
Mediation is often cheaper than litigation for disputes

Disclaimer: This estimate is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. Actual fees vary significantly by attorney, firm, jurisdiction, and case specifics. This tool does not create an attorney-client relationship. Always consult with a licensed attorney for legal advice.
